title: System Design Interview Concepts for Senior Software Engineer
subtitle: An interactive mind map of core topics, sub-topics, and trade-offs.

children.title: Scalability and Load
children.note: How to grow a system to handle more traffic and data.

children.title: Vertical Scalability (Scaling Up)
children.note: **Pros:** Simple — no architecture changes. **Cons:** Hardware ceiling, exponential cost, **Single Point of Failure**.

children.title: Horizontal Scalability (Scaling Out)
children.note: **Pros:** Resilient, theoretically infinite. **Cons:** Requires **stateless** apps, load balancing, network complexity.

children.title: Load Balancers
children.note: Orchestrator that distributes traffic across instances.

children.title: Round Robin
children.note: Simple; ignores current load.

children.title: Least Connections
children.note: Sends to the least busy server — ideal for long sessions.

children.title: IP Hash
children.note: Same user always lands on the same server (sticky sessions).

children.title: Layer 4 (TCP)
children.note: Routes by IP/port — fast, protocol-agnostic.

children.title: Layer 7 (HTTP)
children.note: Routes by URL/headers/cookies — smarter, slightly slower.

children.title: Statelessness
children.note: App layer must be stateless. Move sessions to **Redis / Memcached / DB** so any instance can serve any request.

children.title: CAP and PACELC Theorems
children.note: Trade-offs under network partitions and normal operation.

children.title: Consistency
children.note: Every read returns the latest write or an error.

children.title: Availability
children.note: Every request gets a non-error response.

children.title: Partition Tolerance
children.note: System keeps working despite dropped messages between nodes.

children.title: PACELC
children.note: Else (no partition): trade Latency vs. Consistency.

children.title: Databases and Storage
children.note: Pick the model that fits the access pattern.

children.title: SQL (Relational, ACID)
children.note: Structured data, complex joins, strong consistency. ACID guarantees make these the safe default for money, inventory, and anything with invariants you cannot violate.

children.title: A — Atomicity
children.note: A transaction is all-or-nothing. Partial updates never persist; if any step fails, the whole transaction is rolled back. Example: debit account A, credit account B — both happen or neither.

children.title: C — Consistency
children.note: A transaction takes the database from one valid state to another, respecting all rules (constraints, foreign keys, triggers). The DB never ends up in a state that violates its invariants.

children.title: I — Isolation
children.note: Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other; the result must look as if they ran one after another. Tunable via isolation levels (Read Uncommitted → Serializable) — stronger isolation, lower throughput.

children.title: D — Durability
children.note: Once a transaction commits, it survives crashes, power loss, or restarts. Achieved via write-ahead logs (WAL), fsync, replication.

children.title: Examples
children.note: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle.

children.title: Key-Value
children.note: Redis, DynamoDB — simple lookups, very fast.

children.title: Document
children.note: MongoDB — flexible schemas, JSON-like.

children.title: Columnar
children.note: Cassandra, BigQuery — analytics, wide rows.

children.title: Graph
children.note: Neo4j — relationships first-class.

children.title: Read Replicas
children.note: Scale reads before reaching for sharding.

children.title: Indexes & Query Optimization
children.note: First lever before scaling out.

children.title: Caching Strategies
children.note: Trade freshness for latency at multiple layers.

children.title: Read-through
children.note: App always asks the **cache**; on a miss, the cache itself fetches from the DB, stores the result, and returns it. App stays simple. Risk: first request is slow (cold cache); cache library must know how to query the source.

children.title: Write-through
children.note: Every write goes to **cache and DB synchronously** before returning success. Cache is always fresh, but writes are slower (two round-trips). Good when read-after-write must always hit a warm cache.

children.title: Write-back (Write-behind)
children.note: Writes go to the **cache only**; the cache flushes to the DB asynchronously in batches. Very fast writes and great for write-heavy workloads, but a cache crash before flush = **data loss**. Needs durable cache or an outbox.

children.title: Cache-aside (Lazy loading)
children.note: App checks the cache; on a miss it queries the DB, then writes the value back to the cache. The most common pattern (Redis). App code is in charge — flexible, but easy to get wrong (stale entries, thundering herd on miss).

children.title: Eviction Policies
children.note: When the cache is full, which key gets dropped?

children.title: LRU — Least Recently Used
children.note: Evict the entry untouched for the longest time. Great default — assumes recently-used items will be used again. Used by Redis (`allkeys-lru`), Memcached, most CPU caches.

children.title: LFU — Least Frequently Used
children.note: Evict the entry with the **fewest accesses**. Better when popularity is stable (e.g., a few hot keys dominate). Worse when popularity shifts — old hits keep new entries out.

children.title: TTL — Time To Live
children.note: Each entry has an expiration timestamp; once it passes, the entry is evicted regardless of usage. Use when freshness matters more than popularity (sessions, tokens, rate-limit counters). Often **combined** with LRU/LFU.

children.title: FIFO / Random
children.note: FIFO evicts the oldest insert; Random picks any key. Cheap to implement; rarely a good fit on their own but used as fallbacks.

children.title: Layers
children.note: Caches stack — closer to the user = faster + cheaper.

children.title: CDN (edge)
children.note: Caches static assets (JS, CSS, images, video) in PoPs near users. Dramatic latency wins, offloads origin. Examples: Cloudflare, Fastly, CloudFront. Trade-off: invalidation is harder the further from origin you cache.

children.title: Application (in-memory)
children.note: Cache lives inside the service process or a shared store (Redis, Memcached). Microsecond reads, perfect for hot DB query results, sessions, computed views. Trade-off: consistency between instances + memory cost.

children.title: Database (query/result cache)
children.note: DB engine caches query plans, page buffers, and result sets. Mostly automatic — your job is to give it enough RAM and write good queries. Trade-off: invalidated aggressively on writes; not a substitute for an app-level cache.

children.title: Messaging and Event-Driven Design
children.note: Decouple producers from consumers; absorb spikes; build resilience.

children.title: Architectural comparison — with vs. without Msg/EDD
children.note: The same use case ("a user signs up") looks very different in each style.

children.title: WITHOUT Messaging / EDD (synchronous chain)
children.note: **API → Auth → Email → Billing → Analytics**, all in one request. Pros: trivial to reason about, easy to test, immediate feedback. Cons: tight coupling — if Billing is down the signup fails; latency = sum of all services; spikes overwhelm downstream; hard to add a 6th step without touching the API.

children.title: Symptom
children.note: Cascading failures, retries on the user request, timeouts.

children.title: Fits when
children.note: Small system, few services, low traffic, strong-consistency demand.

children.title: WITH Messaging / EDD (asynchronous events)
children.note: **API → Auth (commits) → publishes `UserSignedUp`**. Email, Billing, Analytics each subscribe and react independently. Pros: producers don't know consumers; queues absorb spikes; one consumer can fail/retry without breaking signup; trivial to add a 6th subscriber. Cons: eventual consistency, harder debugging (distributed traces become essential), need idempotent consumers and a dead-letter strategy.

children.title: Symptom
children.note: Lag in queues, duplicate deliveries — but the core flow stays up.

children.title: Fits when
children.note: Many consumers, spiky load, independent failure domains, event-sourcing or audit needs.

children.title: Queues
children.note: Point-to-point, work distribution (RabbitMQ, SQS).

children.title: Pub/Sub
children.note: Fan-out to many subscribers (Kafka, Pub/Sub).

children.title: Kafka
children.note: Durable log, replay, partitioned consumers.

children.title: RabbitMQ
children.note: Flexible routing, ack semantics, classic broker.

children.title: Async Processing
children.note: Background jobs, retries, dead-letter queues.

children.title: Sharding and Partitioning
children.note: Split a large dataset across nodes to remove the single-DB bottleneck.

children.title: Range-based
children.note: Easy to understand, risk of hot ranges.

children.title: Hash-based
children.note: Even spread, harder range queries.

children.title: Geo / Directory
children.note: Locality and explicit routing.

children.title: Consistent Hashing
children.note: Minimizes re-shuffling when nodes change.

children.title: Hot Partitions
children.note: Watch for skew; salt keys when needed.

children.title: Consistency trade-offs
children.note: Once your data is split across nodes, **strong consistency becomes expensive**. The hard parts are not lookups — they're the operations that span shards.

children.title: Single-shard ops are easy
children.note: If a transaction touches only one shard, that shard's local ACID guarantees still apply — fast and consistent.

children.title: Cross-shard transactions are costly
children.note: Atomic writes across shards need **2-Phase Commit (2PC)** or sagas. 2PC is slow, blocks on coordinator failure, and reduces availability. Most systems avoid it.

children.title: Sagas as a pragmatic alternative
children.note: Replace one big distributed transaction with a sequence of local transactions + **compensating actions** on failure. You buy availability and trade away atomicity — eventual consistency is the new normal.

children.title: Cross-shard reads & joins
children.note: Joining or aggregating across shards = scatter-gather. Latency is bounded by the slowest shard; results may reflect different points in time → **read-your-writes anomalies**.

children.title: Rebalancing (resharding)
children.note: Adding/removing shards moves data while traffic flows. During the move, reads can hit the old or new location — design for double-writes, idempotent migrations, and cutover windows.

children.title: Choosing the shard key matters most
children.note: A bad key creates **hot shards** (all writes to one) or forces every query to fan out. The key should match your dominant access pattern, not what's convenient to index.

children.title: Rule of thumb
children.note: Keep transactional boundaries **inside** a shard; accept eventual consistency **across** shards; expose only idempotent cross-shard operations to clients.

children.title: Microservices and Communication
children.note: Independent services collaborating over the network.

children.title: REST
children.note: Ubiquitous, cacheable, human-friendly.

children.title: gRPC
children.note: Binary, fast, schema-first (Protobuf).

children.title: GraphQL
children.note: Client-driven queries, single endpoint.

children.title: Service Discovery
children.note: **The problem:** in a dynamic system, service instances come and go (auto-scaling, deploys, crashes). Hard-coding `billing-service:8080` doesn't work — that IP/port changes constantly. Service Discovery is how a caller finds **a healthy instance** of a target service at runtime.

children.title: Service Registry
children.note: A live database of `{ service-name → [healthy instance URLs] }`. Each instance **registers itself on startup** ("I'm `billing` at 10.0.1.42:8080") and **deregisters on shutdown**. The registry continuously **health-checks** each instance and removes the dead ones.

children.title: Discovery (the lookup)
children.note: When service A wants to call service B, it asks the registry "give me an instance of B" instead of using a fixed address. The registry returns one (or a list, with load-balancing applied).

children.title: Client-side discovery
children.note: The **caller** queries the registry, picks an instance (with its own load-balancing logic), then calls it directly. Pros: one fewer hop, smart client-side routing. Cons: every client/language needs the discovery library; logic duplicated. Example: Netflix Eureka + Ribbon.

children.title: Server-side discovery
children.note: The caller hits a **fixed address** (a load balancer or API gateway); that component queries the registry and forwards the request. Pros: clients stay dumb, single language-agnostic place to configure routing. Cons: extra hop, the LB itself must be HA. Examples: AWS ALB + ECS, Kubernetes Service + kube-proxy.

children.title: Kubernetes (DNS-based)
children.note: Most teams use this today. Each Service gets a stable internal DNS name (`billing.default.svc.cluster.local`). kube-proxy load-balances across the healthy pods behind it. **You get service discovery for free** by deploying on K8s — no extra component to run.

children.title: Consul
children.note: HashiCorp's standalone registry. Strong health checks, multi-datacenter, KV store, can do DNS or HTTP lookups. Good when you're not on K8s or need cross-cluster discovery.

children.title: Eureka
children.note: Netflix's registry, popular in older Spring Cloud stacks. Client-side discovery model. Largely superseded by K8s and service meshes.

children.title: Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)
children.note: A sidecar proxy next to each pod handles discovery, load-balancing, retries, mTLS, and observability — the application code does nothing. Heaviest option but most powerful.

children.title: Health checks — the unsung hero
children.note: A registry is only useful if it knows what's actually alive.

children.title: Liveness
children.note: "Is the process running?" If it fails, restart the instance.

children.title: Readiness
children.note: "Is the process ready to serve traffic?" (DB connected, cache warm, migrations done.) If it fails, **remove from the pool but don't kill** — the instance is starting up or temporarily overloaded.

children.title: Why senior engineers care
children.note: Service Discovery is the foundation that makes **horizontal scaling, zero-downtime deploys, and self-healing** actually work. Without it, every deploy or autoscale event would break callers.

children.title: API Gateway
children.note: Auth, rate-limit, routing, aggregation.

children.title: Resilience and Fault Tolerance
children.note: Prevent local failures from cascading.

children.title: Circuit Breaker
children.note: Open the circuit on repeated failures, fail fast.

children.title: Retries with Exponential Backoff
children.note: Add jitter; cap attempts.

children.title: Bulkheads
children.note: Isolate resources so one failure can't drown others.

children.title: Timeouts
children.note: Always set them — never block forever.

children.title: Idempotency
children.note: Safe retries for write operations.

children.title: Object Storage and CDNs
children.note: When files don't belong in a database.

children.title: S3-like Object Storage
children.note: Cheap, durable, unstructured blobs.

children.title: CDN
children.note: Edge caching for static assets and media.

children.title: When NOT a DB
children.note: Large media, backups, logs, archives.

children.title: Observability and Monitoring
children.note: You can only fix what you can see.

children.title: Metrics
children.note: Numerical, time-stamped measurements. Cheap to store and aggregate, perfect for dashboards and alerts. Tools: **Prometheus**, Datadog, CloudWatch. Tie them to **SLIs/SLOs** so you alert on user-facing pain, not internal noise.

children.title: RED (for request-driven services)
children.note: Best general framework for HTTP/RPC services.

children.title: Rate
children.note: Requests per second, broken down by endpoint and status.

children.title: Errors
children.note: Failure rate (5xx, exceptions, business errors). Alert on the **ratio**, not the absolute count.

children.title: Duration
children.note: Latency distribution — track **p50, p95, p99**, never just the mean.

children.title: USE (for resources / hardware)
children.note: Best for CPUs, disks, queues, connection pools.

children.title: Utilization
children.note: % of time the resource is busy.

children.title: Saturation
children.note: Backlog / queue depth. The early warning before utilization hits 100%.

children.title: Errors
children.note: Resource-level errors (disk I/O errors, dropped packets, OOM kills).

children.title: Four Golden Signals (Google SRE)
children.note: Almost the same as RED + saturation — what to watch on every service.

children.title: DB connection pool: active / idle / waiting
Business / product metrics
children.note: The ones the business actually cares about — and the strongest signal that something is broken.

children.title: Alert on symptoms, not causes
children.note: Page on "checkout error rate > 1%", not on "CPU 80%".

children.title: Always use percentiles for latency
children.note: Means hide tail latency that hurts real users.

children.title: Label / tag carefully
children.note: By service, endpoint, version, region — but watch cardinality (each unique combo is a time-series).

children.title: Centralized Logs
children.note: ELK, Loki — searchable, structured.

children.title: Distributed Tracing
children.note: Jaeger, OpenTelemetry — request paths across services.

children.title: Alerting
children.note: Page on symptoms (latency, error rate), not causes.

children.title: Decomposition Strategies (Monolith → Microservices)
children.note: How to evolve safely without a Big-Bang rewrite.

children.title: By Functionality
children.note: Cut along the **technical features** of the existing app — "the auth module", "the email module", "the reporting module". Easy to spot from the codebase (one folder = one service). Risk: those modules were drawn for the convenience of developers, not the business — services may share data and constantly call each other, recreating the monolith over the network.

children.title: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
children.note: Cut along **business capabilities** instead of code modules. Each service owns a **Bounded Context** with its own model and language; teams talk to domain experts to find these seams. Harder to discover but produces services that are genuinely independent.

children.title: Starting point
children.note: **Functionality:** the current code structure. **DDD:** the business — events, processes, who-does-what. The boundary is discovered by talking to the people who run the business, not by reading the repo.

children.title: Boundaries
children.note: **Functionality:** technical layers (auth, email, reporting). **DDD:** **Bounded Contexts** — e.g., "Sales", "Shipping", "Billing" — each with its own model. The same word ("Customer") can mean different things in different contexts, and that's allowed.

children.title: Shared model
children.note: **Functionality:** services often share a giant DB or DTOs → invisible coupling. **DDD:** each context owns its data; integration goes through explicit contracts (events, anti-corruption layers). No shared schema.

children.title: Language
children.note: **Functionality:** developer terms. **DDD:** **Ubiquitous Language** — code, docs, and conversations all use the same business vocabulary, avoiding translation bugs between business and engineering.

children.title: Outcome
children.note: **Functionality** often produces a *distributed monolith* — many small services that must deploy together. **DDD** produces **autonomous** services aligned to teams and business goals (Conway's Law working for you, not against you).

children.title: Cost
children.note: DDD is **slower up front** — workshops, event storming, modeling. It pays back when teams can ship independently and the system can evolve without rewriting boundaries.

children.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
children.note: Route slices of traffic to new services; retire old paths gradually.

children.title: Data Decomposition
children.note: Hardest part — decouple shared databases first.
